item.title: The Hidden Price Tag of a Hotter Summer
item.body:
## When Heat Becomes an Economic Story

Every summer arrives with the same headlines about record temperatures, but the more interesting story is increasingly an economic one. Extreme heat is no longer just a weather event — it is a measurable drag on productivity, supply chains, and household budgets.

### Where the Costs Show Up

- **Lost labor hours.** Outdoor work in construction, agriculture, and logistics slows sharply once temperatures climb. Even small productivity losses, multiplied across millions of workers, add up to real GDP effects.
- **Energy strain.** Air-conditioning demand pushes grids toward their limits, raising wholesale electricity prices and, eventually, the bills that land in mailboxes.
- **Food prices.** Heat stress on crops and livestock ripples through grocery costs months later, often after the weather itself has faded from the news.

### Why It Matters for Markets

Central banks and analysts have started treating climate volatility as a genuine input to inflation forecasting rather than background noise. A single severe heat season can nudge food and energy components of the price index, complicating decisions on interest rates.

### The Quiet Adaptation Economy

There is an upside worth watching: spending on cooling technology, heat-resilient infrastructure, and grid upgrades is becoming a durable growth sector. Cities investing early in shade, reflective surfaces, and modernized power systems tend to absorb heat shocks at lower cost.

**The takeaway:** the temperature on the thermometer is also, increasingly, a number on a balance sheet. Reading the summer forecast has quietly become part of reading the economy.
